questions.question: Why is compaction considered part of the architecture rather than a minor background detail?
options: Because if it falls behind, read amplification, latency, and storage debt can become production problems.
Because compaction only matters for document formatting.
Because storage systems can serve reads without touching disk once compaction starts.
Because all deletes are applied synchronously and never need cleanup.
questions.explanation: Maintenance debt directly affects serving quality and cost.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a GC grace period for tombstones?
options: To avoid reclaiming deleted data before lagging replicas or repair processes have safely observed the deletion.
To make storage usage grow forever.
To reduce the need for checksums.
To guarantee zero write amplification.
questions.explanation: Grace periods protect correctness under lag and repair.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that an LSM-backed store is falling behind on maintenance?
options: Compaction debt bytes or read amplification.
The number of markdown headings in docs.
Only total key count.
Daily office badge swipes.
questions.explanation: Debt and amplification show real pressure from incomplete maintenance.

questions.question: Why should destructive lifecycle rules usually support a dry-run mode?
options: To preview impact and catch dangerous retention mistakes before real deletion occurs.
To make all policies permanent immediately.
To avoid writing audit logs.
To remove the need for staged rollout.
questions.explanation: Dry-run reduces the chance of accidental large-scale deletion.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with aggressive compaction settings?
options: They reduce read debt faster, but consume more immediate IO and can hurt foreground latency.
They eliminate the need for replication.
They make lifecycle policies obsolete.
They always lower total cost without risk.
questions.explanation: Aggressive cleanup helps performance later by spending more resources now.

questions.question: What is the real danger of deleting tombstones too early?
options: Lagging replicas may resurrect deleted data or miss the delete altogether.
The cache hit rate becomes too high.
Multipart uploads stop working.
Object IDs lose uniqueness.
questions.explanation: Early reclamation can break delete correctness.

questions.question: Which operational control is most helpful when background work starts hurting the serving path?
options: Adaptive throttling or bandwidth caps for compaction, repair, and lifecycle workers.
Renaming all buckets.
Deleting observability dashboards.
Disabling checksum verification.
questions.explanation: Resource isolation is how you keep maintenance from becoming an outage.

questions.question: Which follow-up most changes the maintenance design?
options: Customers shorten retention dramatically, creating a surge in deletion and transition work.
One dashboard needs a new title.
Object names should allow dashes.
The team wants fewer README lines.
questions.explanation: Large retention changes alter backlog, bandwidth needs, and safety rollout steps.
